articles.title: Config Files That Run Code: Supply Chain Security Blindspot
articles.summary: Config files that run code are often overlooked in supply chain security assessments due to their innocuous nature, but they can be exploited by attackers to inject malicious commands into a developer's workflow. These files typically contain shell commands that are executed when dependencies install or tests run, and can also launch droppers that expose the repository to search engines. The Miasma worm is a notable example of this vulnerability, where an attacker used a config file to launch a credential stealer that exfiltrated AWS, Azure, GCP, Vault, Kubernetes, npm, and GitHub secrets from affected repositories.

articles.title: LLMs and performative productivity

I found that my newfound capabilities allowed me to accomplish more, faster, and with less effort than before, but also led to a lack of understanding about the codebases and user-side changes. The new features I built were neat, but not actually being used, and the greenfield projects quickly abandoned. I questioned whether it was all a net gain, considering I gained little real context or value from my work.

articles.title: Premature Optimization is Fun Sometimes
articles.summary: A connectivity monitoring system is being developed that sends ICMP Echo Requests to multiple servers and monitors latency and dropped packet averages over different time periods. The current data storage approach, using a 512-entry ring buffer with fields for sent and received timestamps, is found to be wasteful, as it requires storing all possible values of these fields. A more efficient solution is proposed by using a tagged union, which stores the relevant information in a single field, reducing memory usage.

articles.title: How much do amd64 microarchitecture levels help in Go?
articles.summary: The Go toolchain exposes a v1 through v4 ladder for AMD64 microarchitecture levels, allowing developers to target older instruction sets while still compiling on newer hardware. This means that a realistic "use everything this CPU offers" target today would need at least a v5, and the entire scheme should be replaced by finer-grained feature detection. The Go toolchain provides a way to set the GOAMD64 environment variable to use everything up to and including AVX2, raising questions about how much gain is actually made from compiling at each level.
